section.title: The Lampstand, the Bread, and the Blasphemer
section.sectionRationale: This chapter pauses the festival calendar to give two ongoing tabernacle duties, the lampstand oil and the bread of the Presence, and then narrates a specific case of blasphemy that establishes equal justice for native Israelite and foreigner alike.
section.thesis: Between Israel's appointed feasts, God maintains a continual light and a continual bread before Him in the tabernacle, and when a man blasphemes His name, He establishes through a real case that His name must be honored and that the same standard of justice applies to native and foreigner alike.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the continual lamp, tended by Aaron every evening; the bread of the Presence, set out weekly and eaten by the priests; and the narrative of the blasphemer, leading to the law of exact retribution, applied equally to all.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Command the Israelites to bring you pure oil of pressed olives for the light, to keep the {{ner|lamps}} burning continually.

Outside the veil of the Testimony in the Tent of Meeting, Aaron is to tend the lamps continually before the LORD from evening until morning. This is to be a permanent statute for the generations to come.
He shall tend the lamps on the pure gold lampstand before the LORD continually.
You are also to take fine flour and bake twelve loaves, using two-tenths of an ephah for each loaf,
and set them in two rows, six per row, on the {{panim|table of pure gold before the LORD}}.
And you are to place pure frankincense near each row, so that it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread, an offering made by fire to the LORD.
Every Sabbath day the bread is to be set out before the LORD on behalf of the Israelites as a permanent covenant.
It belongs to Aaron and his sons, who are to eat it in a holy place; for it is to him a most holy part of the offerings made by fire to the LORD, his portion forever.”

units.label: The blasphemer
units.note: a real case brought to Moses

verses.text: Now the son of an Israelite mother and an Egyptian father went out among the Israelites, and a fight broke out in the camp between him and an Israelite.
The son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the {{shem|Name}} with a curse. So they brought him to Moses. (His mother’s name was Shelomith daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan.)
They placed him in custody until the will of the LORD should be made clear to them.
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Take the blasphemer outside the camp, and have all who heard him lay their hands on his head; then have the whole assembly stone him.
And you are to tell the Israelites, ‘If anyone curses his God, he shall bear the consequences of his sin.

Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD must surely be put to death; the whole assembly must surely stone him, whether he is a foreign resident or native; if he blasphemes the Name, he must be put to death.

units.label: Life for life, one law for all

verses.text: And if a man takes the life of anyone else, he must surely be put to death.
Whoever kills an animal must make restitution, life for life.
If anyone injures his neighbor, whatever he has done must be done to him:
fracture {{tachat|for}} fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. Just as he injured the other person, the same must be inflicted on him.
Whoever kills an animal must make restitution, but whoever kills a man must be put to death.
You are to have the same standard of law for the foreign resident and the native; for I am the LORD your God.’”
Then Moses spoke to the Israelites, and they took the blasphemer outside the camp and stoned him. So the Israelites did as the LORD had commanded Moses.

ner.gloss: The continual lamp, tended nightly so its light never failed. Its unbroken burning before the LORD becomes, in the New Testament, a picture fulfilled in Christ, the light of the world, and in the church, His lampstand, called to shine without ceasing.

panim.gloss: Literally bread of the face or presence, twelve loaves representing Israel's tribes kept continually before God on the golden table. Their unbroken presence pictures a people kept perpetually in fellowship with God, later fulfilled in Christ, the bread of life.

shem.gloss: The Name, used here as a reverent way of referring to the LORD Himself. Blaspheming the Name treats God's own character and reputation with contempt, which is why this single case receives such careful, direct divine instruction.

tachat.gloss: In place of, instead of, or for. This small preposition structures the whole formula of exact retribution, fracture for fracture, eye for eye, establishing a judicial standard of proportional, not excessive, justice for the courts to administer.

basic.context: This chapter interrupts the festival calendar to describe two ongoing tabernacle duties, tending the continual lamp and setting out the weekly bread before the LORD, then tells the story of a man who blasphemed God's name, resulting in a law of exact justice applied equally to native and foreigner.
basic.hermeneutics: The tabernacle furniture and rituals here belong to old covenant ceremonial law fulfilled in Christ; the blasphemer's civil penalty belonged to Israel's specific old covenant order. The enduring principle, reverence for God's name and impartial justice, continues to bind God's people today, even where the specific penalties do not.
basic.originalLanguages: The ner tamid, continual lamp, and the lechem panim, bread of the presence, both picture something kept perpetually before God without lapse. Shem, the Name, names God's own reputation as what the blasphemer defiled. Tachat, in place of, structures the eye for eye formula as a judicial limiting principle, not a license for private revenge.
basic.typology: The continually burning lamp anticipates Christ, the light of the world, and His church as a lampstand; the continually present bread anticipates Christ, the bread of life, who keeps His people perpetually in God's presence.
basic.christ: Jesus says, I am the light of the world, John 8:12, and I am the bread of life, John 6:35, fulfilling in Himself both objects this chapter requires to burn and be set out without fail. Where Israel's lamp and bread had to be constantly tended by human hands, Christ Himself is the unfailing light and sustenance His people now depend on.
basic.god: God cares about the small, repeated tasks of worship, nightly oil, weekly bread, as much as the grand annual feasts, and when His own name is blasphemed, He requires the whole community to take it with the utmost seriousness, while insisting the same law govern native and foreigner alike.
basic.crossReferences: John 8:12 calls Christ the light of the world. John 6:35 calls Him the bread of life. Matthew 5:38-39 addresses the popular misuse of eye for eye as personal license for revenge. Numbers 15:16 echoes this chapter's insistence on one law for native and foreigner.
basic.oneStory: God's unbroken light and bread before Him in the tabernacle, and His insistence on impartial justice for native and foreigner alike, both anticipate the completeness of what Christ provides: an unfailing light and life for His people, and a perfect justice satisfied fully and finally at the cross.

context.literary: This chapter breaks the sequence of festival law in chapters 23 and 25 to insert ongoing tabernacle duties and a specific legal case, showing that Israel's holiness law was not merely theoretical but was actually applied to real situations as they arose.
context.historical: The narrative of the half Egyptian blasphemer reflects Israel's mixed camp during the wilderness years, and Moses' practice of bringing unprecedented cases directly to God for a ruling, a pattern also seen with the daughters of Zelophehad in Numbers.
context.canonical: The lampstand and bread of the Presence recur throughout Scripture's furniture of worship, and the lex talionis principle established here becomes a touchstone Jesus explicitly engages in the Sermon on the Mount.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish that God's continual worship, lamp and bread, must never lapse, and that when His name is blasphemed, real, impartial justice, not partiality toward native over foreigner, must be administered by the whole community.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The tabernacle's specific ritual furniture prescribed here belonged to old covenant ceremonial law, fulfilled in Christ; the blasphemer's narrative and resulting civil penalty belonged to Israel's specific old covenant civil order and are not directly enforced today. The enduring principle, reverence for God's name and impartial justice for all people, continues to bind God's people, even where the specific old covenant penalties do not.

debates.question: Does the lex talionis, eye for eye, in verses 19-20 endorse personal revenge?

views.label: A judicial limiting principle, not a license for private revenge
views.case: Most interpreters read this as instruction to courts, limiting punishment to proportional, not excessive, retribution, guarding against escalating blood feuds, not authorizing individuals to take justice into their own hands.

views.label: Jesus' teaching in Matthew 5:38-39 as a deliberate contrast
views.case: Some see Jesus specifically addressing a popular misuse of this principle as personal license for retaliation, redirecting His followers from personal vengeance toward active nonresistance and love of enemies, without thereby abolishing the state's proper administration of justice, addressed in Romans 13:4.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the text is a judicial standard for courts, not a license for private revenge, and that Jesus' teaching addresses personal conduct between individuals, not the legitimate role of courts and civil authority in administering proportional justice.

study.originalLanguages: The ner tamid, continual lamp, and lechem panim, bread of the presence, share a common concern: something kept unfailingly before God, never allowed to lapse. Shem, the Name, marks the blasphemer's offense as an assault on God's own reputation, not a mere social infraction. Tachat, in place of, gives the eye for eye formula its judicial, proportional logic, matching the punishment precisely to the offense rather than exceeding it.

typology.fulfillment: Christ is the light of the world, and His church, like a lampstand, is called to shine that light without fail, John 8:12, Revelation 1:20.

typology.type: The bread of the Presence, twelve loaves continually before the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ is the bread of life, and through Him His people, pictured in the twelve tribes, are continually kept in God's presence, John 6:35, Hebrews 4:14-16.

Jesus does not abolish justice but fulfills it at the cross, where the exact penalty sin deserves is paid in full, while teaching His followers to leave personal vengeance to God, Matthew 5:38-39, Romans 12:19.

study.christ: Jesus declares, I am the light of the world; whoever follows Me will never walk in darkness, John 8:12, and I am the bread of life; whoever comes to Me shall not hunger, John 6:35, directly fulfilling in Himself the two objects this chapter requires to burn and be set out continually before God. Where Israel's lamp needed nightly oil and its bread needed weekly renewal by human hands, Christ Himself never fails and never needs replacing.
study.god: God cares about the small, repeated, unglamorous tasks of worship, tending oil, setting bread, as seriously as the grand annual feasts, showing that faithfulness in ordinary things matters to Him. When His own name is blasphemed, He requires the whole community to take His honor seriously, while insisting, twice in this chapter, that the very same standard of justice govern native Israelite and foreign resident alike.

crossReferences.note: Jesus calls Himself the light of the world, fulfilling what the continual lamp could only picture.
Jesus calls Himself the bread of life, fulfilling what the bread of the Presence could only picture.
Jesus addresses the popular misuse of eye for eye as personal license for revenge, redirecting His followers toward active nonresistance.
Echoes this chapter's insistence that the same law governs the native Israelite and the foreign resident.

study.oneStory: God's unbroken light and bread before Him in the tabernacle, and His insistence on impartial justice for native and foreigner alike, both anticipate the completeness of what Christ provides: an unfailing light and life for His people, and a perfect justice fully and finally satisfied at the cross for everyone who trusts Him.

quiz.q: Who was responsible for tending the continual lamp, according to verses 3-4?

options.text: Aaron, tending it continually from evening until morning.
options.note: Verses 3-4 name Aaron specifically for this duty.

options.text: Any Israelite who happened to be near the tabernacle.
options.note: The text specifically assigns this duty to Aaron, not the general public.

options.text: The blasphemer named later in the chapter.
options.note: The chapter's structure separates the lamp instructions from the unrelated case of the blasphemer.

options.text: No one is assigned this duty.
options.note: Verses 3-4 explicitly assign this duty to Aaron.

quiz.q: How often was the bread on the golden table renewed, according to verse 8?

options.text: Every Sabbath day.
options.note: Verse 8 specifies this weekly renewal.

options.text: Once a year, on the Day of Atonement.
options.note: Verse 8 specifies a weekly, not yearly, renewal.

options.text: Every single day.
options.note: Verse 8 specifies weekly renewal, not daily.

options.text: No renewal schedule is given.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly gives a weekly schedule.

quiz.q: What was the background of the man who blasphemed the Name in this chapter?

options.text: He was the son of an Israelite mother and an Egyptian father.
options.note: Verse 10 states this directly.

options.text: He was a fully native born Israelite priest.
options.note: Verse 10 specifically identifies his mixed parentage, not priestly status.

options.text: No information about him is given.
options.note: Verse 10 gives specific information about his parentage.

options.text: He was a foreign visitor with no connection to Israel.
options.note: Verse 10 identifies his mother as Israelite, giving him a direct connection to the community.

quiz.q: To whom did God say the law of blasphemy and the law of exact retribution should apply equally?

options.text: Both the foreign resident and the native-born Israelite.
options.note: Verses 16 and 22 both state this explicitly.

options.text: Only to native-born Israelites.
options.note: Verses 16 and 22 explicitly extend the law to foreigners as well.

options.text: Only to priests and Levites.
options.note: The text specifies native and foreigner broadly, not a priestly class specifically.

options.text: No specific group is named.
options.note: Verses 16 and 22 explicitly name both groups.

quiz.q: How does Jesus engage the principle of eye for eye in Matthew 5:38-39?

options.text: He addresses its popular misuse as personal license for revenge, calling His followers to active nonresistance instead.
options.note: Matthew 5:38-39 records this teaching directly.

options.text: He commands that courts abandon all standards of proportional justice.
options.note: Jesus addresses personal conduct between individuals, not the courts' legitimate administration of justice.

options.text: He has no teaching related to this principle.
options.note: Matthew 5:38-39 directly engages this principle.

options.text: He affirms personal revenge as the appropriate response to injury.
options.note: Jesus explicitly redirects His followers away from personal revenge in this teaching.

questions: The lamp and bread required faithful, repeated, often unnoticed maintenance. What unglamorous, repeated disciplines of your own faith need fresh faithfulness this week, even when no one is watching?

The blasphemer's case shows that how we speak of God matters enormously to Him. How careless or reverent is your own speech, whether about God directly or about matters of faith, when you are frustrated or provoked?
God insisted that the very same law govern native Israelite and foreign resident. Where might you be applying a double standard, harsher or more lenient, to people different from yourself?

Jesus is the light of the world and the bread of life, fulfilling what Israel's tabernacle furniture could only picture. Where do you look for light or sustenance apart from Him, and what would it look like to return to Him as your actual source?

The law of eye for eye limited punishment to what was proportional, not excessive. Where do you need to let go of a desire for revenge that exceeds the actual wrong done to you, trusting God's justice instead of taking it into your own hands?

translationNotes.note: Memorial portion translates a term describing the frankincense burned as a token representing the whole offering of bread before the LORD, similar to the memorial portion in grain offerings elsewhere in Leviticus.

Blasphemed the Name with a curse likely describes a specific, deliberate act of cursing using God's own covenant name, YHWH, rather than a vague or general expression of frustration, explaining the case's severity and the need for direct divine instruction.

Fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth is widely understood by both ancient and modern interpreters as a judicial ceiling limiting courts to proportional punishment, rather than a license authorizing private citizens to exact revenge themselves.
